3: Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) or Life Cycle Analysis is a technique to conduct a cradle-to-grave analysis in order to assess and analyze the energy consumption and the environmental impacts coupled with all the phases of the life of a product from raw materials mining throughout the materials processing, manufacturing, sup- ply, use, repair and maintenance, disposal, recycling and reuse. Engineers implement this technique to assist in evaluating the products. The LCA includes the following processes: (1) invento- rying energy and materials inputs and associated outputs to the environment, (2) assessing the impacts associated with inputs and outputs, (3) conducting economic analysis of the whole processes,

and (4) expounding the outcomes to assist in making decisions [1-4]. Additionally, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is one of the most comprehensive and typical methods used for analyzing the envi- ronmental impacts of new technologies, techniques and products. Accordingly, LCA can be used as a mean for estimating the green- house gas (GHG) emissions of different processes within a pro- duction cycle [5].

On the other hand, biogas technology is a powerful mean to recycle livestock manure to produce gaseous fuel (biogas) and biofertilizer through the anaerobic digestion of biomass [6,7]. Anaerobic digestion (AD) is an effective emissions abatement technique to reduce the negative environmental impacts of live- stock manure management. In comparison with other means such as field application of slurry and composting, anaerobic digestion seizes considerable advantages such as producing energy, recycling manure efficiently, and producing biofertilizer [8,9]. Accordingly, a life cycle assessment can be carried out to evaluate the environ- mental impacts and energy balance of biogas production [10]. Several studies conducted life cycle assessment in the field of

biogas production in order to achieve different objectives, such as: (1) Assessment for determining the appropriate period to install biogas systems [11], (2) Assessment of different sizes of biogas plants [9,12,13], (3) Environmental assessment of biogas plants [3,12,14-17], (4) Economic analysis of power generation [4,15], (5) Assessment of different technologies for biogas purification and upgrading [18,19], (6) Assessment of biogas production from different substrates [5,10,20,21], and (7) Assessment of resulting digestate [13,22]. Lijó et al. [23] conducted LCA of electricity pro- duction from anaerobic co-digestion of pig slurry and energy crops. Cremiato et al. [24] conducted LCA to investigate the effect of anaerobic digestion, materials recovery and secondary fuels pro- duction on the environmental impact of municipal solid waste management.

Several studies deal with the effects of light on the enhancement of biogas production. Yang et al. [25] concluded that an enhance- ment in biogas production by illumination with incandescent lamps at a light intensity of 5000 lux. The improved activity of the methanogens in the digester under an optimum illumination time can explain this enhancement. Tada and Sawayama [26] indicated that light at a wavelength between 390 and 540 nm enhanced the methane production from thermophilic anaerobic digestion. Furthermore, Tada et al. [27] stated that the presence of light at wavelength between 400 and 540 nm stimulates the methyl- coenzyme M [(2-(methylthio) ethanesulfonic acid (CH3-SCoM))] to form CH4.

The state-of the-art focuses on the biostimulation of microor- ganisms. In addition, photoactivation has been found to bio- stimulate and, therefore, enhance the activity of the microorganisms. Abdelsalam et al. [28,29] concluded that the irradiation of rumen fluid with 532 nm laser source enhanced the anaerobic process and accelerate the manure digestion, which in- creases the biogas and methane production. It is well known that; laser light has three distinct characteristics that distinguish it from ordinary light which are; monochromatic, directional, and coherent. These characteristics enable laser light to gain a great energy within a very small area.

Photobiostimulation of anaerobic bacteria through different exposure times within the process of anaerobic digestion of live- stock manure which maximized the biogas and methane produc- tion, minimized the lag phase, and shortened the required time to achieve the highest biogas and methane production. Accordingly, several questions and issues have been raised: (1) Generally, is this an environmentally-friendly technology? (2) Precisely, which irradiation time length would be the most environmentally- friendly compared to all other treatments? (3) What would be the environmental performance of this technology? and (4) Further several questions regarding the energy consumption compared to the increased energy production using this technology. Therefore, the main objective of this study is to conduct an environmental impact evaluation of the use of 532 nm laser radiation in biogas production from anaerobic digestion of livestock manure.

Accordingly, several questions and issues have been raised: (1) Generally, is this an environmentally-friendly technology? (2) Precisely, which irradiation time length would be the most environmentally- friendly compared to all other treatments? (3) What would be the environmental performance of this technology? and (4) Further several questions regarding the energy consumption compared to the increased energy production using this technology. Therefore, the main objective of this study is to conduct an environmental impact evaluation of the use of 532 nm laser radiation in biogas production from anaerobic digestion of livestock manure.
9: The aim of this study was to present the specific impacts on different environmental indicators mitigation of producing and utilizing biogas as an energy source.

The biogas production and energy generation were calculated

Where, the irradiation setup con- sists of either laser source (250 mW, Nd:Y second harmonic, 532 nm, China) or incandescent light source (i.e. 200 W incandes- cent lamp), magnetic hot plate stirrer (SCILOGEX, MS7-H550-S, Connecticut, USA), and beam expander to expand the laser beam diameter to 0.01 m. 10: The standardized methodology of life cycle assessment (LCA) was followed using the GaBi® 6.0 tool (thinkstep AG, Germany). Therefore, useful energy was used as a functional unit. Other environmental impacts were also assessed but the results are omitted in this publication. The system boundaries are illustrated in Fig. 1. 11: For characterizing the impact of the biogas systems on global warming, the main GHGs carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) were taken into account, using CO2 equivalence factors (mass basis) for a 100 year time horizon with a global warming potential (GWP) of 298 for N20 (without considering climate-carbon cycle feedback) and 25 for CH4. Accordingly, the results of Abdelsalam et al. [28,29] concerning the use of laser ra- diation in biogas production from livestock manure were used in the calculations carried out in this study and were estimated on year basis. For each exposure time length, a biogas production calculation was carried out based on the specifications [32] of standard biogas plants in Europe. Dependent on the values of biogas produced through each variant/scenario, the overall energy, heat energy and electrical energy were further calculated.
12: In order to investigate the acidification, eutrophication, Fresh- water ecotoxicity, ozone layer depletion potential, and human toxicity potential as well as to calculate the specific greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions of electricity and heat generation from biogas.
13: Allocation of the total system GHG emissions between heat and power output has been a topic for never ending discussion. Subsequently, the transmembrane electric potential of mitochondria and activates ATP synthesis will increase. The results of this study show that the use of any of the variants/ scenarios causes nearly the same amounts emissions in the different environmental indicators. In all the scenarios the biogas produced from manure (standard) was the worst case in all Envi- ronmental indicators.

However, Abdelsalam et al. [28] recommended the use of 0.5 h laser irradiation over all other treatments and all other variants due to the fact that the use of 0.5 h laser irradiation increases the pro- duction of biogas and methane in rates higher than those of the other treatments. Furthermore, it was found that the energy applied for the shorter irradiation time by laser (0.5 was the minimal among the different irradiation time. Precisely, the energy applied during the different irradiation times were 111.1, 36.1, 72.2 and 144.4 kWh when RF was irradiated with 1 h light, 0.5, 1 and 2 h laser, respectively. The biogas and methane production found to be inversely proportional to the exposure time to 532 nm laser source. Moreover, Yin et al. [35] concluded that the cumulative biogas production yielded from dairy manure with dark fermentation was higher than the photo fermentation by sunlight, where it could be explained by the results of Zhang et al. [36] who stated that not all the emission spectrum of sunlight is suitable for bacteria. In contrast, an observation of biogas production found with pig manure, which explains that appropriate light and duration can promote anaerobic fermentation of pig manure [37]. These obser- vations can clarify the inversely proportional of biogas production with laser irradiation time. Fortunately, this study shows that there are no significant dif- ferences among all treatments in terms of the environmental impact. In this study, the environmental impact was evaluated by considering the greenhouse gas emissions, global warming po- tential, biogas utilization (energy consumption), biogas production and net energy. This methodology agrees with the methodology applied by Nasution et al. [5]; where greenhouse gas emissions were further considered by Nikkhah et al. [11] as the main indices for the installation of biogas production systems. On the other hand, electricity generation was considered to be a part of the function of the biogas system, where the other part is heat gener- ation. This is more applicable and mimics the real biogas plants and, therefore, this approach disagrees with that applied by Ruiz et al. [4] which admirably assumed that power generation is the only function of the biogas system to allow expanding the boundaries of the system to include more functions for their study. The concept of this study was to apply a comparative life cycle assessment to evaluate the life cycle environmental impacts of using laser radiation in the biogas production process in order to demonstrate the avoided greenhouse gas emissions in CO2-equiv- alent, where the same concept was previously adopted by Pérez- Camacho et al. [3].

This methodology agrees with the methodology applied by Nasution et al. [5]; where greenhouse gas emissions were further considered by Nikkhah et al. [11] as the main indices for the installation of biogas production systems. On the other hand, electricity generation was considered to be a part of the function of the biogas system, where the other part is heat gener- ation. This is more applicable and mimics the real biogas plants and, therefore, this approach disagrees with that applied by Ruiz et al. [4] which admirably assumed that power generation is the only function of the biogas system to allow expanding the boundaries of the system to include more functions for their study. The concept of this study was to apply a comparative life cycle assessment to evaluate the life cycle environmental impacts of using laser radiation in the biogas production process in order to demonstrate the avoided greenhouse gas emissions in CO2-equiv- alent, where the same concept was previously adopted by Pérez- Camacho et al. [3].
